title: Digital's VAX: Alive and Kicking With TPC Benchmarks
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: This Viewpoint section analyzes Digital Equipment Corporation's March 1992 VAX product-line price-performance realignment, using TPC-A benchmark data to show that Digital simultaneously rationalized pricing across its entire VAX range—from MicroVAX 3100 to VAX 6000—while achieving the industry's best price-performance at the entry level. Aberdeen concludes that Digital has deliberately repositioned the VAX as a commercial price-performance leader, tripling cost-efficiency since 1990 and becoming competitive not only with HP and IBM but also with emerging RISC vendors such as Sequent.
importance_rationale: As one section of Aberdeen's pioneering TPC benchmark Viewpoint publication series, this document represents early independent analyst use of TPC benchmarks as the primary currency of commercial computing competitive analysis, helping to establish TPC benchmarks as an industry standard evaluation tool.
relevance_rationale: The specific VAX product line and 1992 pricing are entirely obsolete; the document's value is historical, illustrating price-performance dynamics in the minicomputer-to-RISC transition era.
prescience_rationale: While Kastner accurately assessed the VAX's short-term price-performance competitiveness, the longer-term trajectory—the VAX being supplanted by Alpha and eventually phased out—was already implied by the Alpha successor context mentioned in the document.
